meta_title: For Students: SaaS pricing to Solve can't finish projects (Beginner Resource Page)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS pricing and overcome can't finish projects. A Beginner-level Resource Page using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: SaaS Pricing to Solve Project Abandonment
content.seo_description: Stop abandoning projects. Master SaaS pricing strategies and ship faster using Antigravity and Claude Code. The ultimate guide for student developers.
content.answer_block: The root cause of project abandonment is scope creep disguised as 'perfecting' features. Implementing a tiered SaaS pricing model early forces you to prioritize core value delivery over vanity features. By defining a paid 'Starter' tier, you constrain your scope, focus on shipping, and validate your build with real users immediately.
content.intro:
You’ve been here before: a blank IDE, a brilliant idea, and three weeks later, a graveyard of half-finished repositories. The excitement fades, the complexity mounts, and your 'killer app' becomes another forgotten folder on your desktop. It isn't a lack of talent; it's a lack of constraints.

Real-world SaaS engineering isn't about building everything; it's about building the right thing. By integrating a pricing strategy into your development lifecycle, you transform your project from a never-ending hobby into a focused product. Let’s stop the cycle of abandonment and start shipping software that provides measurable value.

steps.title: Define the Core Value Proposition
steps.description: Identify the single problem your app solves for a specific user. If you can't describe the value in one sentence, your scope is too broad.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Act as a senior product architect. Help me define a 'Minimum Viable Value' for my SaaS project. I need to distill my idea into one core feature that justifies a subscription.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'analyze project requirements and identify the singular core feature'

steps.title: Structure Your Pricing Tiers
steps.description: Create a tiered structure (Free/Pro/Enterprise) to force feature prioritization. Only the 'Pro' tier gets the complex bells and whistles.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a JSON schema for a SaaS pricing model. Include three tiers: Free, Starter ($9/mo), and Pro ($29/mo). Map the features to these tiers.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'generate pricing-model.json with feature mapping based on user role'

steps.title: Build the Authentication Gate
steps.description: Implement authentication early. This acts as the technical boundary for your pricing tiers.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Scaffold a secure authentication flow using Supabase or Clerk. Integrate a middleware check to verify user subscription status before accessing protected routes.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'implement auth middleware to restrict access based on subscription_tier'

steps.title: Integrate Stripe Billing
steps.description: Connect your app to Stripe to make the pricing real. Handling real money changes your mindset from 'student' to 'founder'.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a backend service to handle Stripe checkout sessions. Include logic to update the user's database status upon successful payment.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'setup stripe webhook handler for subscription_updated events'

steps.title: Ship the 'Starter' MVP
steps.description: Deploy only the features required for the Starter tier. Use the remaining planned features as a roadmap for future updates.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Review my current codebase and identify files that are not essential for the Starter tier. Suggest a plan to move these into a 'Phase 2' branch.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'refactor project to exclude non-MVP features and prepare for deployment'

pro_tips: Always build the payment integration before the UI; it ensures you never build a product that can't be monetized.
Use feature flags to toggle 'Pro' features; this allows you to ship a single codebase that adapts to user subscription status.
Limit your MVP development time to 14 days; if it takes longer, your scope is still too big.
Treat your GitHub commit history as a resume; focus on shipping small, incremental features rather than massive, buggy updates.
Don't worry about perfect design in the beginning; focus on the 'Time to First Value' for your user.
content.next_steps: Once your MVP is live, share it with three potential users and gather feedback. Do not add new features until you have your first 10 beta testers.
content.conversion_block: Stop letting your brilliant ideas die in a hidden repository. By applying professional pricing logic, you gain the focus needed to transform your code into a real business. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
